title: Enthalpy change, ΔH
summary: 9701 AS energetics — exothermic and endothermic reactions, calorimetry, and ΔH notation with PhET Energy Forms and Changes.
learningObjectives: Define enthalpy change ΔH and distinguish exothermic from endothermic reactions.
Use q = mcΔT in calorimetry calculations.
Interpret energy profile diagrams and balanced thermochemical equations.
Apply standard conditions and correct sign conventions for ΔH.

simpleExplanation.title: Heat in, heat out
simpleExplanation.summary: Enthalpy change measures heat transferred at constant pressure. Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH negative); endothermic reactions absorb energy (ΔH positive).
steps: Exothermic: products have less energy than reactants — heat released to surroundings.
Endothermic: products have more energy — heat absorbed from surroundings.
Calorimetry: measure temperature change, use q = mcΔT, scale to per mole using equation.
Always include state symbols and balance equations in thermochemical work.

sections.content: **Enthalpy** is central to 9701 Topics 5–6. Cambridge tests **sign conventions**, **calorimeter calculations**, and **energy cycle** reasoning that builds into Hess's law (5.2).
q = mcΔT | ΔH = −q (system perspective, exothermic ΔH < 0) | kJ mol⁻¹ per equation as written

sections.question: When 0.500 g of propane burns, temperature of 200 g water rises by 12.5 °C. Calculate heat absorbed by water. (c = 4.18 J g⁻¹ K⁻¹)
sections.solution:
q = mcΔT = 200 × 4.18 × 12.5 = **10 450 J** = 10.45 kJ

(Combustion is exothermic — this energy is transferred to the water.)

sections.content: Show **sign of ΔH** explicitly. Exothermic combustion has **ΔH negative** even though the surroundings (water) gain heat.
